categories.definition: Practical value elements that solve everyday problems Values in this tier: Saves time, Simplifies, Makes money, Reduces effort, Reduces cost, Reduces risk, Organizes, Integrates, Connects, Quality, Variety, Informs, Avoids hassles, and Sensory appeal.

Value elements that create feelings and emotional connections Values in this tier: Reduces anxiety, Rewards me, Nostalgia, Design/aesthetics, Badge value, Wellness, Therapeutic value, Fun/entertainment, Attractive appearance, and Provides access.
Value elements that transform customer lives Values in this tier: Provides hope, Self-actualization, Motivation, Heirloom, and Affiliation/belonging.
Value elements that benefit society Values in this tier: Self-transcendence.

elements.definition: Saving time in tasks or transactions
Reducing complexity and simplifying
Helping to make money
Getting things done with less effort
Saving money in purchases, fees or subscriptions
Protecting from losses
Becoming more organized
Integrating different aspects of life
Connecting with other people
Providing high-quality goods or services
Providing a variety of things to choose from
Providing reliable and trusted information about a topic
Avoiding or reducing hassles
Appealing in taste, smell, hearing and other senses
Helping people worry less and feel more secure
Providing benefits for being a loyal customer
Reminding people of something positive in the past
Providing an appealing form or design
Representing achieved status or aspirations
Improving people's physical or mental state
Providing therapeutic value or well-being
Offering fun or entertainment
Helping people feel more attractive
Providing access to information, goods, services or other valuable items
Providing something to be optimistic about
Providing a sense of personal accomplishment or improvement
Spurring people to achieve their goals
A good investment for future generations
Helping people become part of a group or identify with people they admire
Helping other people or society more broadly

categories.definition: Must-have elements for market entry Values in this tier: Meeting specifications, Acceptable price, Regulatory compliance, and Ethical standards.
Functional value elements for business operations Values in this tier: Improved top line, Cost reduction, Product quality, Scalability, and Innovation.

subcategories.definition: Economic groups Improved top line and Cost reduction — a Bain subcategory whose score represents those 2 value elements.
Performance groups Product quality, Scalability, and Innovation — a Bain subcategory whose score represents those 3 value elements.

categories.definition: Elements that make doing business easier Values in this tier: Time savings, Reduced effort, Decreased hassles, Information, Transparency, Organization, Simplification, Connection, Integration, Availability, Variety, Configurability, Responsiveness, Expertise, Commitment, Stability, Cultural fit, Risk reduction, Reach, Flexibility, and Component quality.

subcategories.definition: Productivity groups Time savings, Reduced effort, Decreased hassles, Information, and Transparency — a Bain subcategory whose score represents those 5 value elements.
Operational groups Organization, Simplification, Connection, and Integration — a Bain subcategory whose score represents those 4 value elements.
Access groups Availability, Variety, and Configurability — a Bain subcategory whose score represents those 3 value elements.
Relationship groups Responsiveness, Expertise, Commitment, Stability, and Cultural fit — a Bain subcategory whose score represents those 5 value elements.
Strategic groups Risk reduction, Reach, Flexibility, and Component quality — a Bain subcategory whose score represents those 4 value elements.

categories.definition: Personal benefits for decision-makers Values in this tier: Network expansion, Marketability, Reputational assurance, Design & aesthetics, Growth & development, Reduced anxiety, and Fun & perks.

subcategories.definition: Career groups Network expansion, Marketability, and Reputational assurance — a Bain subcategory whose score represents those 3 value elements.
Personal groups Design & aesthetics, Growth & development, Reduced anxiety, and Fun & perks — a Bain subcategory whose score represents those 4 value elements.

categories.definition: Elements that inspire and motivate Values in this tier: Vision, Hope, and Social responsibility.

subcategories.definition: Helps the customer understand and fulfill its organizational purpose

elements.definition: Conforms to the customer's internal specifications
Provides products or services at an acceptable price
Complies with regulations
Performs its activities in an ethical manner
Helps the customer increase revenue
Reduces cost for the customer's organization
Provides high-quality goods or services
Expands easily to additional demand, processes or tasks
Provides innovative capabilities
Saves time for users or the overall organization
Helps an organization get things done with less effort
Helps the customer avoid unnecessary hassles
Helps users become informed
Provides a clear view into the customer's organization
Helps users become more organized
Reduces complexity and keeps things simple
Connects organizations and users with others internally and externally
Helps a customer integrate different facets of the business
Ensures the good or service is available when and where needed
Provides a variety of goods or services to choose from
Offers goods or services that can be easily configured to the customer's needs
Responds promptly and professionally to my organization's needs
Provides know-how for the relevant industry or market
Shows it is committed to the customer's own success
Is a stable company for the foreseeable future
Fits well with the customer's culture and people
Protects the customer against loss or unnecessary risk
Allows the customer to operate in more locations or market segments
Moves beyond standard goods or services to allow customization
Improves the perceived quality of the customer's own products or services

Helps users and colleagues develop personally
Helps buyers and others in the organization feel more secure
Is enjoyable to interact with or otherwise rewarding
Helps the customer anticipate the direction of its markets
Gives buyers and users hope for the future of their organization
Helps the customer be more socially responsible

categories.definition: People with Strategic Thinking strengths absorb and analyze information that informs better decisions. Themes: Analytical, Context, Futuristic, Ideation, Input, Intellection, Learner, and Strategic.
People with Executing strengths make things happen. Themes: Achiever, Arranger, Belief, Consistency, Deliberative, Discipline, Focus, Responsibility, and Restorative.
People with Influencing strengths help a team reach a broader audience. Themes: Activator, Command, Communication, Competition, Maximizer, Self Assurance, Significance, and Woo.

People with Relationship Building strengths build strong relationships that hold a team together and make it greater than the sum of its parts. Themes: Adaptability, Connectedness, Developer, Empathy, Harmony, Includer, Individualization, Positivity, and Relator.

elements.definition: People exceptionally talented in the Analytical theme search for reasons and causes. They have the ability to think about all of the factors that might affect a situation.
People exceptionally talented in the Context theme enjoy thinking about the past. They understand the present by researching its history.
People exceptionally talented in the Futuristic theme are inspired by the future and what could be. They energize others with their visions of the future.
People exceptionally talented in the Ideation theme are fascinated by ideas. They are able to find connections between seemingly disparate phenomena.
People exceptionally talented in the Input theme have a need to collect and archive. They may accumulate information, ideas, artifacts or even relationships.
People exceptionally talented in the Intellection theme are characterized by their intellectual activity. They are introspective and appreciate intellectual discussions.
People exceptionally talented in the Learner theme have a great desire to learn and want to continuously improve. The process of learning, rather than the outcome, excites them.
People exceptionally talented in the Strategic theme create alternative ways to proceed. Faced with any given scenario, they can quickly spot the relevant patterns and issues.
People exceptionally talented in the Achiever theme work hard and possess a great deal of stamina. They take immense satisfaction in being busy and productive.

People exceptionally talented in the Arranger theme can organize, but they also have a flexibility that complements this ability. They like to determine how all of the pieces and resources can be arranged for maximum productivity.
People exceptionally talented in the Belief theme have certain core values that are unchanging. Out of these values emerges a defined purpose for their lives.
People exceptionally talented in the Consistency theme are keenly aware of the need to treat people the same. They crave stable routines and clear rules and procedures that everyone can follow.
People exceptionally talented in the Deliberative theme are best described by the serious care they take in making decisions or choices. They anticipate obstacles.
People exceptionally talented in the Discipline theme enjoy routine and structure. Their world is best described by the order they create.
People exceptionally talented in the Focus theme can take a direction, follow through and make the corrections necessary to stay on track. They prioritize, then act.
People exceptionally talented in the Responsibility theme take psychological ownership of what they say they will do. They are committed to stable values such as honesty and loyalty.
People exceptionally talented in the Restorative theme are adept at dealing with problems. They are good at figuring out what is wrong and resolving it.
People exceptionally talented in the Activator theme can make things happen by turning thoughts into action. They want to do things now rather than simply talk about them.
People exceptionally talented in the Command theme have presence. They can take control of a situation and make decisions.
People exceptionally talented in the Communication theme generally find it easy to put their thoughts into words. They are good conversationalists and presenters.
People exceptionally talented in the Competition theme measure their progress against the performance of others. They strive to win first place and revel in contests.
People exceptionally talented in the Maximizer theme focus on strengths as a way to stimulate personal and group excellence. They seek to transform something strong into something superb.

People exceptionally talented in the Self-Assurance theme feel confident in their ability to take risks and manage their own lives. They have an inner compass that gives them certainty in their decisions.

People exceptionally talented in the Significance theme want to make a big impact. They are independent and prioritize projects based on how much influence they will have on their organization or people around them.
People exceptionally talented in the Woo theme love the challenge of meeting new people and winning them over. They derive satisfaction from breaking the ice and making a connection with someone.
People exceptionally talented in the Adaptability theme prefer to go with the flow. They tend to be "now" people who take things as they come and discover the future one day at a time.
People exceptionally talented in the Connectedness theme have faith in the links among all things. They believe there are few coincidences and that almost every event has meaning.
People exceptionally talented in the Developer theme recognize and cultivate the potential in others. They spot the signs of each small improvement and derive satisfaction from evidence of progress.
People exceptionally talented in the Empathy theme can sense other people's feelings by imagining themselves in others' lives or situations.
People exceptionally talented in the Harmony theme look for consensus. They don't enjoy conflict; rather, they seek areas of agreement.
People exceptionally talented in the Includer theme accept others. They show awareness of those who feel left out and make an effort to include them.

People exceptionally talented in the Individualization theme are intrigued by the unique qualities of each person. They have a gift for figuring out how different people can work together productively.
People exceptionally talented in the Positivity theme have contagious enthusiasm. They are upbeat and can get others excited about what they are going to do.
People exceptionally talented in the Relator theme enjoy close relationships with others. They find deep satisfaction in working hard with friends to achieve a goal.

categories.definition: "Very few people or companies can clearly articulate WHY they do what they do. When we ask WHY, we're not referring to making money—that's a result. Your WHY is your purpose, cause, or belief. WHY does your company exist? WHY do you get out of bed every morning? And WHY should anyone care about the work you do?"

"Companies and people know HOW they do what they do and can typically communicate these processes clearly. HOWs are often utilized to explain that a product or service is better than a competitor's—but HOWs can be so much more than that.

"Every single company and organization on the planet knows WHAT they do. This is true no matter how big or small, and no matter what industry. Everyone is easily able to describe the products or services a company provides or the job function they have within that system. WHATs are easy to identify and important to have, but are less impactful than the WHY."

The deepest layer of the Golden Circle analysis that focuses on understanding the people and relationships that give purpose its meaning. The WHO goes beyond the traditional Golden Circle to create deeper, more sustainable connections.

elements.definition: How clear and well-articulated is the WHY on the website?
Does copy reflect true beliefs rather than generic marketing?
Does the site inspire and motivate visitors?
Is WHY messaging consistent across pages and sections?
Is the WHY unique and meaningful vs competitors?
Does WHY copy connect emotionally with the audience?
What makes the HOW distinctive in copy?
How clearly defined are methods and processes?
Is HOW described consistently across the site?
Does HOW copy show support for the stated WHY?
Are there proof points demonstrating HOW?
Does copy suggest a durable competitive advantage?
How clear are product/service offerings?
Do offerings align with stated WHY and HOW?
Is quality signaled in WHAT copy?
Does WHAT copy prove value with evidence?
Does copy show innovation while staying on-mission?
Does WHAT copy show fit with market needs?
How clearly is the target audience defined?
Does copy attract people who share the WHY?
Is the audience specific rather than “everyone”?
Does copy show deep understanding of audience needs?
Will the target audience feel the message is for them?
Does copy foster loyalty and advocacy?

categories.categoryName: Ego Archetypes (Leave a Mark on the World)
categories.definition: Ego Archetypes (Leave a Mark on the World) groups The Hero, The Magician, and The Outlaw — brands in this motivational cluster share related narrative identity patterns.
Order Archetypes (Provide Structure) groups The Caregiver, The Ruler, and The Creator — brands in this motivational cluster share related narrative identity patterns.
Freedom Archetypes (Yearn for Paradise) groups The Innocent, The Explorer, and The Sage — brands in this motivational cluster share related narrative identity patterns.
Social Archetypes (Connect with Others) groups The Regular Guy/Girl, The Jester, and The Lover — brands in this motivational cluster share related narrative identity patterns.

Differentiation gaps vs competitors in messaging
Emerging revenue opportunities signaled in content
